claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 18 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine whether you're eligible for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with a basic question:

**How many children are you inquiring about for Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm inquiring about one child - my son Alex. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'm here to help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Before I proceed with my assessment, let me first review the official eligibility criteria so I can give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, I've reviewed the official eligibility criteria. Now I need to gather some information about your specific circumstances to determine whether you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 18 years old, I need to check a few more things to determine eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. I have one more important question:

**Do you live with Alex, or does he live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for clarifying that. I have just one final question:

**Are you, or your partner if you have one, earning an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000 a year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me review the rules carefully:

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed:
- You can normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and live in the UK
- However, you'll **normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training**
- You are responsible for a child if you live with them (which applies in your case)
- The high income child benefit tax charge applies if either you or your partner earn over £60,000, but this doesn't affect eligibility - only whether you pay tax on it

Since Alex is 18 years old and is currently studying for his A-levels (which is approved education), and you live with him, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex!**

Here's why:
- Alex is 18 years old and under 20
- He is in approved education (A-levels)
- You live with him, making you responsible for him
- The rules state that you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training

You should be able to make a claim with HMRC. If you need to make a claim or have any further questions, you can contact the Child Benefit Office.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is currently in approved education (studying for A-levels). According to the eligibility rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. You live with Alex, so you are responsible for him. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets the criteria as he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels), you live with him, and neither you nor your partner earns over £60,000 (which would only trigger a tax charge, not affect eligibility)."

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and is currently in approved education (studying for A-levels). According to the eligibility rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. You live with Alex, so you are responsible for him. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets the criteria as he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels), you live with him, and neither you nor your partner earns over £60,000 (which would only trigger a tax charge, not affect eligibility)."
